name: plan
context: Preview infrastructure changes with terraform plan, save plan files, and interpret diff output
info: The `terraform plan` command creates an execution plan showing what Terraform will do to reach the desired state defined in the configuration. It reads the current state (from the configured backend), compares it to the configuration, and produces a detailed diff of resources to create, update, or destroy. Plan is a read-only operation — it makes no changes to real infrastructure. The output shows each resource action with `+` for create, `~` for update, `-` for destroy, and `-/+` for replace. Plans can be saved to binary files with `-out` for later exact application with `terraform apply`. Variables can be supplied via `-var`, `-var-file`, or environment variables. The `-target` flag restricts planning to specific resources. The `-refresh-only` flag detects drift without proposing config-driven changes. The `-destroy` flag previews what a full destroy would do. Machine-readable JSON output is available with `-json` for integration with automation tools and policy engines like OPA or Sentinel.
body:
# terraform plan

`terraform plan` compares the desired state in your `.tf` configuration to the actual state of deployed resources and shows exactly what will change. No infrastructure is modified — plan is purely a read/diff operation.

## Basic Usage

```bash
# Plan in the current directory
terraform plan

# Plan with non-interactive mode
terraform plan -input=false

# Plan and save to a file for exact apply later
terraform plan -out=tfplan

# Plan a different directory
terraform -chdir=environments/staging plan
```

## Plan Output Format

Terraform uses symbols to indicate the action for each resource:

| Symbol | Action | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `+` | create | New resource will be created |
| `-` | destroy | Existing resource will be destroyed |
| `~` | update in-place | Resource will be modified without recreation |
| `-/+` | replace | Resource must be destroyed and recreated |
| `<=` | read | Data source will be read during apply |

```
# Example plan output
Terraform will perform the following actions:

  # aws_instance.web will be updated in-place
  ~ resource "aws_instance" "web" {
      ~ instance_type = "t3.micro" -> "t3.small"
        tags          = {
            "Name" = "web-server"
        }
    }

  # aws_s3_bucket.logs will be created
  + resource "aws_s3_bucket" "logs" {
      + bucket = "my-app-logs-2026"
      + id     = (known after apply)
      + arn    = (known after apply)
    }

Plan: 1 to add, 1 to change, 0 to destroy.
```

Values shown as `(known after apply)` are computed by the cloud provider during resource creation.

## Saving Plan Files

Save a plan to guarantee the exact changes are applied:

```bash
# Save the plan
terraform plan -out=tfplan

# View the saved plan in human-readable format
terraform show tfplan

# View the saved plan as JSON
terraform show -json tfplan | jq .

# Apply the exact saved plan
terraform apply tfplan
```

Saved plan files are binary and encode the exact set of changes. This is the safest way to ensure what you reviewed is what gets applied, especially in CI/CD.

## Variable Input

```bash
# Set a single variable
terraform plan -var="instance_type=t3.large"

# Set multiple variables
terraform plan -var="region=us-west-2" -var="env=staging"

# Use a variable file
terraform plan -var-file=environments/prod.tfvars

# Use multiple variable files
terraform plan -var-file=common.tfvars -var-file=prod.tfvars

# Variables via environment
export TF_VAR_region=us-east-1
export TF_VAR_instance_type=t3.micro
terraform plan
```

### Variable File Format

```hcl
# prod.tfvars
region         = "us-east-1"
instance_type  = "t3.large"
environment    = "production"
min_instances  = 3
enable_logging = true

tags = {
  Team    = "platform"
  Project = "core-infra"
}
```

## Targeted Plans

Restrict the plan to specific resources with `-target`:

```bash
# Plan only a specific resource
terraform plan -target=aws_instance.web

# Plan an entire module
terraform plan -target=module.vpc

# Multiple targets
terraform plan -target=aws_instance.web -target=aws_security_group.web
```

Targeted operations are a last resort — they can leave state inconsistent. Prefer full plans whenever possible.

## Drift Detection with -refresh-only

Detect changes made outside Terraform (manual console edits, other tools):

```bash
# Detect drift without proposing config changes
terraform plan -refresh-only

# Apply the refresh to update state to match reality
terraform apply -refresh-only
```

This is useful for auditing infrastructure drift and reconciling state without modifying resources.

## Destroy Preview

Preview what a destroy operation would do:

```bash
# Preview full destruction
terraform plan -destroy

# Preview targeted destruction
terraform plan -destroy -target=aws_instance.web

# Save destruction plan
terraform plan -destroy -out=destroy.tfplan
```

## Machine-Readable Output

JSON output for automation, policy engines, and custom tooling:

```bash
# Stream JSON plan output
terraform plan -json

# Save plan then export as JSON
terraform plan -out=tfplan
terraform show -json tfplan > plan.json

# Parse with jq — list all resources being changed
terraform show -json tfplan | jq '.resource_changes[] | {address: .address, action: .change.actions}'

# Count changes by action type
terraform show -json tfplan | jq '[.resource_changes[].change.actions[]] | group_by(.) | map({action: .[0], count: length})'
```

## Common Flags Reference

| Flag | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `-out=FILE` | Save plan to a binary file for exact apply |
| `-var="key=value"` | Set an input variable value |
| `-var-file=FILE` | Load variables from a .tfvars file |
| `-target=ADDR` | Restrict plan to specific resource/module |
| `-refresh-only` | Detect drift without config-driven changes |
| `-destroy` | Preview destruction of resources |
| `-refresh=false` | Skip state refresh (use cached state) |
| `-replace=ADDR` | Force replacement of a specific resource |
| `-input=false` | Disable interactive variable prompts |
| `-json` | Machine-readable JSON output |
| `-no-color` | Disable terminal color codes |
| `-parallelism=N` | Limit concurrent provider operations (default 10) |
| `-compact-warnings` | Condense warning output |

sections.title: Plan output format
sections.body: Terraform plan uses + for create, - for destroy, ~ for update in-place, -/+ for replace (destroy then create), and <= for data source reads. Each resource block shows the attribute-level diff with before and after values. Values marked (known after apply) are computed server-side. The summary line at the bottom reports total counts: N to add, N to change, N to destroy.

sections.title: Saving and applying plan files
sections.body: Use -out=FILE to save a plan. The saved file is a binary that encodes the exact changes. Apply it with terraform apply FILE — no re-planning occurs. Use terraform show FILE to inspect in human format or terraform show -json FILE for machine-readable output. Saved plans are the safest approach for CI/CD: plan in one step, review, then apply the saved file.

sections.title: Variable input
sections.body: Variables can be set via -var flags, -var-file for .tfvars files, environment variables (TF_VAR_name), or auto-loaded files (terraform.tfvars, *.auto.tfvars). Precedence from lowest to highest: defaults in variable blocks, terraform.tfvars, *.auto.tfvars, -var-file, -var flags, environment variables. Use .tfvars files per environment (dev.tfvars, prod.tfvars) for clean separation.

sections.title: Targeted plans
sections.body: The -target flag restricts planning to a specific resource address or module. Useful for debugging or applying urgent fixes to a single resource. Multiple -target flags can be combined. Targeted operations skip dependency validation for non-targeted resources, which can lead to inconsistent state. HashiCorp recommends targeted operations only as a last resort.

sections.title: Drift detection with -refresh-only
sections.body: The -refresh-only flag causes Terraform to refresh the state from real infrastructure and report any differences without proposing configuration-driven changes. This detects drift caused by manual edits, other tools, or out-of-band changes. Apply with -refresh-only to update the state file to match reality. Combine with scheduled CI runs for continuous drift monitoring.

sections.title: Machine-readable JSON output
sections.body: The -json flag produces structured JSON output suitable for policy engines (OPA, Sentinel), custom dashboards, and automation scripts. The JSON plan format includes resource_changes with before/after values, prior_state, planned_values, and configuration metadata. Use terraform show -json planfile for the richest output. Parse with jq to extract specific change details.

links.context: Parent CLI node with global flags and environment variables
Init must be run before plan to install providers and configure backend
Apply executes the changes previewed by plan
Plan -destroy previews what terraform destroy would do

related.note: Init must be run before plan
Apply executes the plan; saved plans guarantee exact changes

scripts.name: Terraform Plan with Output File
scripts.description: Run terraform plan and save the result to a plan file, then display a summary of changes
scripts.inline:
set -euo pipefail
DIR="${TF_WORKING_DIR:-.}"
PLAN_FILE="${PLAN_OUT:-tfplan}"
PLAN_ARGS="-input=false -out=$PLAN_FILE"

# Variable files
if [ -n "${VAR_FILE:-}" ]; then
  for vf in $(echo "$VAR_FILE" | tr ',' '\n'); do
    PLAN_ARGS="$PLAN_ARGS -var-file=$vf"
  done
fi

# Individual variables
if [ -n "${VARS:-}" ]; then
  for v in $(echo "$VARS" | tr ',' '\n'); do
    PLAN_ARGS="$PLAN_ARGS -var=$v"
  done
fi

# Target
if [ -n "${TARGET:-}" ]; then
  for t in $(echo "$TARGET" | tr ',' '\n'); do
    PLAN_ARGS="$PLAN_ARGS -target=$t"
  done
fi

# Destroy preview
if [ "${DESTROY:-false}" = 'true' ]; then
  PLAN_ARGS="$PLAN_ARGS -destroy"
fi

# Refresh-only
if [ "${REFRESH_ONLY:-false}" = 'true' ]; then
  PLAN_ARGS="$PLAN_ARGS -refresh-only"
fi

echo "Running: terraform -chdir=$DIR plan $PLAN_ARGS"
terraform -chdir="$DIR" plan $PLAN_ARGS
echo '' && echo '=== Plan Summary (JSON) ===' && terraform -chdir="$DIR" show -json "$PLAN_FILE" | jq '{resource_changes: [.resource_changes[]? | {address: .address, actions: .change.actions}]}'

TF_WORKING_DIR.description: Working directory with .tf files (default: current directory)

PLAN_OUT.description: Output file name for saved plan (default: tfplan)

REFRESH_ONLY.description: Set to 'true' for drift detection only

directory.description: Path to the Terraform working directory

outFile.description: File path to save the binary plan

refreshOnly.description: Drift detection mode — refresh state only

hasChanges.description: Whether the plan includes any resource changes

planFile.description: Path to the saved plan file if -out was used

resourceChanges.description: List of resource changes with their actions

depends.reason: Init must be run before plan
